By Shawn Twing
Before we get into the details about scaling your paid traf�c
engine let’s start with a controversial idea — scaling paid traf�c
may not be your most important priority.
What?
How can that be?
Aren’t you the paid traf�c guy? Here me out (and please don’t ask
André for a refund).
Optimizing and scaling traf�c should be viewed as activities in
service of a higher-level goal — optimizing and scaling your
business.
Even that isn’t entirely accurate.
Do you want to scale your business or scale your revenue?
Do you want to scale your revenue or your income?
Exactly.
Optimizing and Scaling
www.getwsodo.com

-- 1 of 18 --

So lesson #1 in this section is that scaling (paid) traf�c and scaling
your income are not necessarily the same thing. We have to keep
our eyes on the prize.
Two ways you could scale revenue and income without scaling
paid (or organic) traf�c are selling more to your existing
customers and selling to those customers at higher price points.
(Leverage shows up to the party when you do both.)
These are not my areas of expertise and I’m going to stay in my
lane.
However, knowing that you have a �nite amount of time to
invest in your business means that every choice must be
evaluated in terms of opportunity costs. What could you be
doing instead of scaling paid traf�c?
I’m de�nitely not suggesting that you don’t scale paid traf�c.
My point in this quick detour is to keep everything that follows in
context. I can show you how to scale paid traf�c — YOU need to
decide if that’s where your time and energy is best spent right
now.
That’s a question you’ll return to periodically as your answers will
change as your business and its performance evolves.
In my experience, consistent, predictable sources of high-quality
visitors, prospects, and customers are valuable to every business
www.getwsodo.com

-- 2 of 18 --

so let’s move on from here assuming you have decided that’s a
worthwhile area to focus your attention.
Find Your “Bright Spots”
The most common (and ridiculously annoying) advice I hear
about how to scale paid traf�c is “just do more of what works”.
Right. More of what works — got it. That’s brilliant…
When someone says “do more of what works” I hear “I don’t really
know what’s working so I’m going to keep pouring money in and
hope that more money = more customers.”
Before I let my natural tendencies toward sarcasm get away from
me, let me admit (with some humility) that there is a kernel of
truth in the “do more of what works” strategy.
But, it’s the difference between simple and simplistic. What
follows is simple once you wrap your mind around it, and it
avoids the simplistic “more of what works” trap.
In their (amazing) book Switch: How to Change Things When
Change Is Hard, brothers Dan and Chip Heath wrote about the
importance of “bright spots” to identify meaningful, impactful
opportunities for positive change.
For our purposes, a bright spot is something that signi�cantly
www.getwsodo.com

-- 3 of 18 --

outperforms the norm.
Audience segments are the �rst place to look:
▪ Are there audience segments that perform 2-3x better than others?
▪ Or, if the segments you’re able to observe don’t show any obvious
bright spots, what about your customers as a group?
▪ If you interviewed 20, 30, 50, 100 of your customers what do you
think you’d notice?
▪ What do you already notice?
▪ Have they had similar life experiences?
▪ Do they share a similar triggering event that a�ected their
transition from prospect to customer?
Look past the obvious triggering events.
Some are only the price of entry.
For example, if your audience is brides to be and you’re targeting
women who have changed their FB status to engaged, yes, that’s a
triggering event.
But those are table stakes — it doesn’t tell you what else is
happening that turns someone from a group of similar prospects
to someone who then becomes your customer.
Look deeply and when you have customers to ask, ASK!
www.getwsodo.com

-- 4 of 18 --

Notice I didn’t say survey. Yes, you can do that, and yes, you’ll get
interesting and potentially valuable results. If you’re going to use
a survey early on, keep it short and include a question that asks,
“May I contact you directly by phone or email?”
We’re not looking for statistical signi�cance, we’re looking for
CLUES. We can follow those clues in conversation much better
than we can in survey data.
The example I shared earlier of a client who o�ered a much larger size
for their product wasn’t a question in a survey — that came from
listening to customers and pulling on some of the threads that
emerged in conversation.
We could spend days talking about where and how to �nd these
bright spots.
So much of that depends on your unique business, your
customers, and your IDEAL customers. Use your imagination and
really take this exercise seriously.
Look at the objective, quanti�able data you have and then dig
deeper and deeper and deeper.
When you �nd something that looks like a bright spot (minimum
2x better performance), you have two goals — isolate it, and then
expand upon what you isolated.
www.getwsodo.com

-- 5 of 18 --

Often that requires reallocating resources.
Google AdWords Example
Here are two examples for how to think about this — one for
AdWords and one for Facebook (both are real client examples).
One of my consulting clients manages their own AdWords
account.
When I �rst started working with them their budget was
allocated politically, not �nancially ($9,000/month divided into
three, $3,000 parts — one for each of their business units). Within
each of those business units all of their product lines were given
an equal share of that $3,000 budget.
It was impossible to see any bright spots when we analyzed data
daily/weekly.
There were sales, but those sales weren’t consistent because the
budgets were spread thin and they ran out quickly every day.
Each business unit sliced and diced its budget to make sure they
had covered all of their products, and everyone on the executive
team was convinced AdWords was a waste of money because it
was barely breaking even some months and losing money other
months.
I was not hired to help this client scale their AdWords account. I
www.getwsodo.com

-- 6 of 18 --

was hired to justify shutting it down.
The owner had a sense that some things could work, but he didn’t
know what those things were and he had lost patience talking
about ideas that never produced results.
The �rst thing I did was look at data for an entire year.
That perspective changed everything.
Viewed daily or weekly the trends weren’t visible.
Viewed annually from a variety of perspectives (business unit,
product line, top selling products, highest margin products, time
of day, day of week, geography, etc.) different information began
to emerge.
They had a very small number of high-margin products that sold
the most volume, and those products sold at two distinct times of
day in three states.
Once the performance data was visible, the constraints to that
performance became obvious very quickly.
Their individual “bright spots” had less than 10% search
impression share meaning their ads showed up less than 10% of
the time someone in their ideal geographic locations at their ideal
selling times of day was searching.
www.getwsodo.com

-- 7 of 18 --

Why?
Because their budget was being consumed by dozens of other
campaigns running the rest of the day across the rest of the
country for products that weren’t selling (in areas that weren’t
buying).
The solution was easy to implement and radically effective.
We allocated all of their budget for ads to a limited number of
high-margin products that displayed in three states during �ve
speci�c hours of the day.
Within three weeks we had 90%+ search impression share
(meaning their ads were seen 90% of the time of more when
someone searched for those products in one of those three states
during those two windows of time during the day).
Within 60 days of that change this client went from barely
breaking even to 8.5x return on ad spend.
That’s the exponential power of bright spots in action.
Let’s move on to a Facebook example.
Facebook Example
One of the most overlooked opportunities with Facebook
advertising is the EQUITY that’s built up over time within a
www.getwsodo.com

-- 8 of 18 --

properly structured Facebook account.
Facebook’s ability to create custom audiences based on user
behavior, and then use their big data and machine intelligence to
�nd lookalike audiences is immensely powerful.
Let’s look at some scenarios and then explain how to use those
scenarios to scale campaigns.
In lesson six I mentioned the importance of behavioral signals in
the context of an MPPS — for example, clicking through from
page one to page two, opting in, etc.
Let’s expand on this idea beyond just an MPPS and see how we
can use it in different ways.
Imagine our business has a core website, a blog, and we have
created our �rst MPPS with an opt-in as a goal.
Our core product is an online meditation and self-improvement
program and our blog has three broad categories of content —
stress relief (problem-focus), personal improvement (lifestyle
focus), and meditation (solution-focus).
Facebook loves content so our �rst awareness campaign
identi�es a large audience and we promote our blog content to
that audience as a whole — one ad set is focused on stress-relief,
another on self-improvement, and the third on meditation.
www.getwsodo.com

-- 9 of 18 --

The imagery and copy for each ad reinforces that particular topic,
and a click on a stress-relief ad lands on a stress-relief blog article,
self-improvement ads leads to a self-improvement article, and
meditation leads to — you guessed it — a meditation article.
The action we care about (and the action we’ll use for our scaling
efforts) isn’t the click — it’s the consumption of the content.
That’s what we would optimize our campaign for because we
want to tell Facebook what we care about is the audience that sees
our individual blog posts.
Now let’s assume we run this campaign for a couple of weeks and
generate 5,000 pageviews of our blog content — 3,000 for stress
relief, 1,000 for self-improvement, and 1,000 for meditation.
We’ve just learned our �rst important lesson — all things being
equal with the audience, stress relief gets attention 3x more than
our other topics.
If we hadn’t written our MPPS yet this information would give us a
starting point to frame that content.
Now that we’ve given Facebook some data to work with we can
use that data for our bene�t.
Facebook looks at the 3,000 visitors who saw the stress relief
www.getwsodo.com

-- 10 of 18 --

landing page and creates a lookalike audience of people with
similar characteristics.
A small segment of that (a 1% lookalike) most closely matches the
characteristics of the 3,000 visitors who saw our content. Now we
have another audience to target that Facebook found on our
behalf.
We then repeat the process for the other two audiences (self-
improvement and meditation).
That’s three new audiences from our �rst audience, and each
audience has a narrower focus (stress relief, self-improvement,
and meditation).
For the sake of our example, let’s assume that we have the time,
energy, and dollars to create an MPPS for each of these audiences.
One is the entry point into our world for the stress relief crowd,
one is for the self-improvement crowd, and the third is for
meditation.
The content of each MPPS is informed by our knowledge of what
attracted that person into our world in the �rst place.
Let’s look at what we have built for equity up to this point. We
have one large audience we’ve identi�ed on Facebook to start
with.
www.getwsodo.com

-- 11 of 18 --

From that audience we have 5,000 people who have seen our
content, showing interest in three different categories.
That has given us six opportunities.
Three of those opportunities are advertising our MPPS to each of
the 5,000 people who have seen our content already (framed in the
most appropriate context).
Three more opportunities are audiences Facebook has identi�ed
on our behalf that most closely match those initial 5,000 people.
What do we do next?
We now advertise our MPPS to the 1% lookalikes by theme and we
optimize those campaigns for �rst to second page conversions.
Why?
You guessed it — we’re going to build new audiences (and more
equity) from people who are taking the action we care about
(consuming our MPPS content past page one).
Now we have three new audiences of people who are even more
re�ned and more valuable (because we’re giving Facebook
increasingly granular, behaviorally-based inputs to work with).
Now imagine we’ve run traf�c to this particular three-part traf�c
engine for long enough to generate 1,000 opt-ins (500 stress relief,
www.getwsodo.com

-- 12 of 18 --

250 self-improvement, and 250 meditation).
That means we have even more valuable data for Facebook to use
because these new audiences have taken one of the actions we
care about most — they have opted in!
But we’re still not �nished. Over time our customer list will grow
as well and that behavioral signal can be used by Facebook to �nd
new prospects.
(If you already have a customer list you can upload that to Facebook as a
custom audience to start.)
The cycle repeats over and over again, increasingly informed by
the signals that matter to your business beginning with the most
general (consumed your content thematically) to more speci�c
(page one to page two conversion), to even more speci�c (opted in)
and �nally to monetization (made a purchase).
When we structure our thinking and our campaigns so that we
create clear, high-quality signals for Facebook to use we can
leverage its machine intelligence and big data on our behalf,
building equity every step of the way.
This is the bright spots strategy in action tailored speci�cally for
the capabilities that Facebook provides.
We are telling Facebook where to look for the bright spots by
identifying the actions that matter to our business.
www.getwsodo.com

-- 13 of 18 --

At �rst those actions are broad — for example, the consumption of
content. Over time they become increasingly meaningful to our
business (and increasingly effective on our behalf).
Google Display Network
In lesson one I said that I believe in both/and not either or. So let’s
add some jet fuel to your overall scaling strategy with both/and.
We’ll do that by replicating your Facebook audience strategy on
the Google Display Network.
[Most of the ads you see on websites other than Facebook are inventory
managed by the Google Display Network (GDN). That’s where your
Google retargeting ads show up. (There are other ways to use the GDN but
let’s stay focused on our scaling strategy.)]
Google Analytics creates the Facebook equivalent of custom
audiences which can be used to advertise on the GDN. The default
audience is “all site visitors” which means anyone who visits a
page on your site with the Google Analytics tracking code, no
matter what they do.
If we were to replicate our Facebook scaling strategy the �rst
thing we would do is create three page and time-based audiences.
Those would be:
▪ Audience #1: visited the stress relief blog post and stayed longer
than 10 seconds.
www.getwsodo.com

-- 14 of 18 --

▪ Audience #2: visited the self-improvement blog post and stayed
longer than 10 seconds.
▪ Audience #3: visited the meditation blog post and stayed longer
than 10 seconds.
Later, when we have an MPPS for each of these topics we would
create another three audiences:
▪ Audience #1: visited page-two of our stress relief MPPS.
▪ Audience #2: visited page-two of our self-improvement relief
MPPS.
▪ Audience #3: visited page-two of our meditation MPPS.
Over time, we would build additional audiences for opt-ins
(de�ned by pageview in Google Analytics of onclick events in
Google Tag Manager if there isn’t a page destination) and
purchases.
Each of these audiences represents equity as well.
We can interact with each audience because we know the criteria
to be part of that audience.
For example, if we know someone has seen some of our blog
content we know the next step is a MPPS. If that person has seen
page two of our MPPS we know we’re nurturing them toward the
opt-in and then the purchase.
www.getwsodo.com

-- 15 of 18 --

We’re doing this simultaneously in Facebook and on the Google
Display Network which means we’re visible across 85% of the
Internet!
Think about that for a moment.
Someone has been exposed to your corner of the world and then,
based on that person’s current depth of involvement with you (as
signaled by their behavior) you’re gently guiding and nurturing
them one step at a time through your entire value-delivery
system.
You’re simultaneously using Facebook and Google’s machine
intelligence and big data on your behalf to �nd more prospects
who are very similar to your current prospects, leads, and
customers, and then interacting with each of those people in a
way that’s congruent with their exposure to and self-expressed
interest to your business.
The positive feedback loop created systematically scales your
results intelligently 24/7/365.
The end result is that you have created a machine that effectively
builds itself based on the quality of the inputs you’re giving it.
Time to Take Action
That’s a lot to digest and I encourage you to come back to this
www.getwsodo.com

-- 16 of 18 --

lesson many times.
The �rst priority is setting up the signals that will allow your
business to scale intelligently. Once those have been setup, you’ll
act on those gradually over time as those options become
available.
Once you have created your MPPS you’ll want to setup the criteria
for Facebook and Google to track audiences that see page two,
page three, page four (etc. for each of the pages in our MPPS).
You’ll also want to specify audiences that take the �nal action in
your MPPS (opt in, schedule a demo, schedule a consultation, etc.).
And, �nally, specify audiences that take purchasing actions (if you
offer different products/service create an audience for all
purchases and separate audiences for speci�c individual purchase
by product/service).
Think about the various audiences as containers for information
that are being �lled over time.
You may not have time to act on all of them immediately and
that’s perfectly OK. I want you to have the option to act on them
BEFORE you need them.
Audiences are not created retroactively which means you
need to have de�ned the audiences you care about early and
then let them populate over time.
www.getwsodo.com

-- 17 of 18 --

It’s a little extra work that pays enormous dividends over time.
My intention when I started writing the content for the paid
traf�c section of Sphere of In�uence was to share with you
principles and ideas that are timeless.
That meant focusing more on strategy and a way of thinking vs.
tactics.
However, everything I have described in the previous eight
lessons requires ACTION to deliver value to you and your
customers. I realize it’s overwhelming to consider all at once. The
good news is you don’t have to.
I wish you the best of luck in this journey and I am VERY excited
to hear about your successes.
Remember — it’s not bragging if you did it!
—Shawn Twing
www.getwsodo.com

-- 18 of 18 --
